author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
Not Every Sin is Pride. How Pride is the Commencement of Every Sin.
title: Not Every Sin is Pride. How Pride is the Commencement of Every Sin.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: How Pride is the Commencement of Every Sin.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: “But how,” asks he, “shall we separate pride itself from sin?”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Now, why does he raise such a question, when it is manifest that even pride itself is a sin?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: “To sin,” says he, “is quite as much to be proud, as to be proud is to sin; for only ask what every sin is, and see whether you can find any sin without the designation of pride.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Then he thus pursues this opinion, and endeavours to prove it thus: “Every sin,” says he, “if I mistake not, is a contempt of God, and every contempt of God is pride.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For what is so proud as to despise God?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: All sin, then, is also pride, even as Scripture says, Pride is the beginning of all sin.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Let him seek diligently, and he will find in the law that the sin of pride is quite distinguished from all other sins.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For many sins are committed through pride; but yet not all things which are wrongly done are done proudly,—at any rate, not by the ignorant, not by the infirm, and not, generally speaking, by the weeping and sorrowful.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: And indeed pride, although it be in itself a great sin, is of such sort in itself alone apart from others, that, as I have already remarked, it for the most part follows after and steals with more rapid foot, not so much upon sins as upon things which are actually well done.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: However, that which he has understood in another sense, is after all most truly said: “Pride is the commencement of all sin;” because it was this which overthrew the devil, from whom arose the origin of sin; and afterwards, when his malice and envy pursued man, who was yet standing in his uprightness, it subverted him in the same way in which he himself fell.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For the serpent, in fact, only sought for the door of pride whereby to enter when he said, “Ye shall be as gods.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Truly then is it said, “Pride is the commencement of all sin;” and, “The beginning of pride is when a man departeth from God.”
